output:
# Research Analysis: Repeated-Exposure Reliance Confound (RERC)

Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field Machine Learning.

The dominant method for evaluating whether AI explanations help humans achieve appropriate reliance on AI predictions uses a two-phase, same-instance measurement design: participants first judge instance X unaided, then re-judge the SAME instance X with AI advice and explanation. The resulting reliance metric -- agreement-based appropriate-reliance rate, switch fraction, or weight-of-advice -- is read as evidence that explanations calibrate human reliance. This paper identifies and decomposes a measurement artifact in that design: having already committed to a judgment on instance X in phase-1 creates anchoring, commitment-and-consistency pressure, and mere-exposure familiarity that distort the phase-2 reliance reading independently of the explanation's informational content. We name this the Repeated-Exposure Reliance Confound (RERC) and promote the single two-phase scalar to a design-controlled, commitment-decomposed appropriate-reliance profile with three components: R (genuine explanation-driven revision, estimated from a between-subject fresh-instance arm where each participant encounters each instance exactly once with AI advice and no phase-1 anchor), P (prior-commitment artifact, the increment caused by same-instance repetition), and RxP (the commitment-by-disagreement interaction, concentrated on the human-AI disagreement cases the field scrutinizes most, separately labeled and never folded into a main effect). The decomposition is anchored by a by-construction collapse identity: under the within-subject same-instance, committed-and-recorded, grand-mean-marginalized limit, the R/P/RxP profile recovers the original two-phase scalar as recomputed within floating-point tolerance. A graded commitment manipulation (committed-and-recorded / elicited-but-private / exposed-only-no-judgment) and a dual-placebo design (anchor-destroying scrambled-instance Leg-1, advice-absent Leg-2) make each component independently falsifiable, while a pre-committed refutation criterion ensures that a null result validates the existing measurement convention rather than leaving the question open.

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

The explainable-AI (XAI) program for decision support rests on a deceptively clean premise: expose why a model predicts, let a human decide when to follow it, and the human-AI team should outperform either party alone. Adadi and Berrada's 2018 survey [1] codified this interpretability turn and its promise of calibrated, transparent assistance. Early empirical work made the dependent variable explicit by instrumenting explanations for their effect on trust and reliance in real decision settings. Lai and Tan 2019 [2] tested whether explanations of a deception-detection model helped workers spot lies, reporting modest accuracy gains but also warning that saliency-style rationales sometimes misdirected attention. Bansal, Wu, Zhou, Fok, Nushi, Kamar, Ribeiro, and Weld 2021 [3] sharpened the aspirational target to COMPLEMENTARY team performance -- the whole exceeding its parts -- measured on the team's joint decisions while the AI and its explanation remained on screen. Their influential finding was that explanations can raise human acceptance of WRONG AI advice just as readily as correct advice, undermining the assumption that transparency calibrates reliance.

The response was a pivot from raw reliance -- how often does the human follow the AI? -- to APPROPRIATE reliance -- does the human follow correct advice and reject incorrect advice? Schemmer, Hemmer, Kuehl, Bartelt, and Satzger 2022 [4] and Schemmer, Kuehl, Benz, Bartelt, and Satzger 2023 [5] operationalized this with agreement-based metrics: Relative AI Reliance (the rate at which a participant follows correct AI advice) and Relative Self-Reliance (the rate at which a participant overrides incorrect advice). Switch fraction -- the proportion of instances on which the participant changes their initial answer after seeing AI advice -- and weight-of-advice (Harvey and Fischer 1997 [6]) provide complementary continuous measures. Together, these scalars became the dominant dependent variable in the XAI decision-support subfield, replacing earlier unidimensional trust questionnaires.

Yet even with a better target, the empirical picture remained mixed. Bucinca, Malaya, and Gajos 2021 [7] showed that only cognitive-forcing interventions -- designs that deliberately inject friction before the human can accept AI advice -- curbed overreliance, at a cost to user satisfaction. A meta-analysis by Schemmer, Hemmer, Nitsche, Kuehl, and Vossing 2022 [8] found the effect of explanations on decision quality to be, at best, inconsistent across studies. Bansal et al. [3] themselves documented overreliance: participants armed with explanations were no better at rejecting wrong AI predictions than participants without them. Schemmer, Kuehl, Benz, and Satzger 2022 [26] extended this line by showing that XAI can even amplify automation bias under certain conditions. The subfield has been left with a "mixed-results crisis," in which ostensibly similar designs yield contradictory conclusions about whether explanations improve, harm, or leave unchanged the appropriateness of human reliance on AI.

This paper asks whether part of that inconsistency is methodological rather than substantive -- specifically, whether a structural feature of the dominant measurement design introduces a confound that varies across studies and inflates or deflates the reported reliance effect in unpredictable directions. The structural feature is the TWO-PHASE, SAME-INSTANCE design: the participant first judges instance X unaided (phase-1), then re-judges the SAME instance X with AI advice and explanation (phase-2). The appropriate-reliance metric is read off the phase-2 verdict. This "estimate-then-revise" paradigm is not idiosyncratic to any single study; it is the field's default. It descends from the Judge-Advisor System (JAS) paradigm in behavioral decision research (Sniezek and Buckley 1995 [9]; Bonaccio and Dalal 2006 [10]): a judge forms an initial estimate, receives advice, and revises. The XAI variant substitutes an AI model for the human advisor and adds an explanation. Weight-of-advice, the most studied continuous measure of advice utilization (Yaniv 2004 [11]; Yaniv and Kleinberger 2000 [12]), is defined explicitly as a function of the judge's initial and final estimates, requiring a pre-advice anchor by construction. The two-phase same-instance structure is therefore built into the measurement algebra itself.

The psychology of judgment and decision-making offers several well-established mechanisms that predict a systematic artifact in this design. The most direct is anchoring-and-adjustment (Tversky and Kahneman 1974 [13]). A participant who has just committed to an answer on instance X in phase-1 carries that answer as a numeric or categorical anchor into phase-2; subsequent adjustment toward the AI's advice is insufficient, a pattern documented in hundreds of anchoring studies and reviewed by Furnham and Boo 2011 [14]. In the XAI setting, the participant's phase-1 judgment is a self-generated anchor -- known to be particularly potent because it is personally meaningful and cognitively available (Epley and Gilovich 2001 [15]). Second, commitment-and-consistency (Cialdini 2001 [16]; Deutsch and Gerard 1955 [17]) predicts that having made a public or recorded commitment to a judgment in phase-1, the participant will resist changing it in phase-2 even when new evidence (the AI advice plus explanation) warrants revision. The strength of this effect depends on whether the phase-1 judgment was merely contemplated, privately recorded, or publicly committed -- a gradient the source paper's design does not manipulate. Third, mere-exposure (Zajonc 1968 [18]) contributes a familiarity increment: the second encounter with instance X is processed more fluently, and processing fluency is attributed to the quality of the stimulus rather than to prior exposure. In an XAI context, this could make the instance seem "easier" the second time, inflating the participant's confidence in their own phase-1 judgment.

A directly relevant near-miss in the XAI literature is the work of Rastogi, Arber, Giang, Cibin, Guo, Banovic, Riedl, and Lease 2022 [19], which studied anchoring in human-AI teams under the framing "Deciding Fast and Slow." They showed that seeing the AI's prediction first anchors the human's subsequent judgment on it, reducing decision quality when the AI is wrong. Crucially, Rastogi et al. model anchoring on the AI ADVICE -- the anchor is the machine's output. RERC targets a different anchor: the participant's OWN phase-1 judgment, which is created by the measurement DESIGN rather than by the AI. The two anchoring sources are distinct and can co-exist: in a standard two-phase trial, the participant is anchored both on their own phase-1 answer (RERC) and on the AI advice shown in phase-2 (Rastogi et al.). Neither prior work separates nor quantifies the design-induced anchor from the advice-induced anchor. Gajos and Mamykina 2022 [20] asked a related upstream question -- "Do People Engage Cognitively with AI?" -- and provided evidence that many participants process AI explanations superficially, raising the possibility that the phase-2 reliance reading reflects shallow acquiescence rather than deep belief revision. Ehsan and Riedl 2020 [21] situated this in a broader call for human-centered XAI, arguing that explanation evaluation must account for the cognitive and social context of the user. Neither, however, traced the measurement-design pathway from phase-1 commitment to phase-2 reliance metric contamination.

Research methodology has long recognized the pattern. Campbell and Stanley 1963 [22] catalogued "testing" -- the reactive effect of a pretest on post-test scores -- as a canonical threat to internal validity in within-subject designs. Shadish, Cook, and Campbell 2002 [23] formalized it as a "testing threat": when participants are exposed to the same instrument twice, their second response is distorted by memory, practice, or anchoring from the first. The parallel is exact: in the two-phase same-instance XAI design, phase-1 is the pretest and phase-2 is the post-test; the "treatment" is the AI advice and explanation. The XAI reliance metric is therefore confounded by the testing threat in precisely the way Campbell and Stanley described. Experimental economists have taken this seriously: Charness, Gneezy, and Kuhn 2012 [24] systematically compared within-subject and between-subject designs and showed that within-subject designs can inflate or deflate treatment effects through demand characteristics, anchoring, and order effects. Orne 1962 [25] coined the term "demand characteristics" for the cues that convey experimental hypotheses to participants; in a same-instance two-phase XAI study, the hypothesis is transparent -- "we want to know if the AI explanation changed your mind" -- and participants may adjust their phase-2 response accordingly, either toward or away from the AI advice.

Despite this convergence of established psychology, the testing-effect threat has NOT been ported to the XAI appropriate-reliance metric as a formal decomposition. Several papers flag repeated exposure as a limitation. The source paper notes that its design "may have strengthened participants' prior intuition." Bucinca et al. [7] note within-subject carryover concerns. But flagging a limitation is cheap: none of these papers QUANTIFIES the artifact, decomposes the reliance metric into a design-clean component and a design-induced component, or proposes a falsifiable test for the magnitude of the confound. This is the empty cell that RERC fills. RERC does not merely critique the two-phase design; it decomposes the reliance scalar into R (genuine explanation-driven revision, measured from a design-clean fresh-instance arm), P (the prior-commitment artifact, estimated as the increment caused by same-instance repetition), and RxP (the interaction of prior commitment with human-AI disagreement status, concentrated on precisely the items the field scrutinizes most). The decomposition is constructed so that under the within-subject same-instance limit, the profile collapses by construction to the original two-phase scalar, ensuring backward compatibility with existing results.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

**Spine-Fit Gate verdict.** This is an empirical human-subjects measurement paper that instruments participants making predictions on high-stakes decision tasks, exposes them to AI predictions with explanations, and reads a verdict -- "explanations help achieve appropriate reliance" -- off a behavioral scalar (agreement-based appropriate-reliance rate, switch fraction, weight-of-advice). The scalar is computed from a two-phase, same-instance design: phase-1 collects the participant's unaided judgment on instance X, then phase-2 shows the same instance X with AI advice and explanation. It is NOT a method/system paper proposing a new algorithm to extend. We apply the eval-confound decomposition spine: the load-bearing measurement is self-confounded by the measurement DESIGN itself -- re-judging the same instance creates a prior commitment/anchor that distorts the phase-2 reliance reading independently of the explanation. The source explicitly flags this: its design "may have strengthened, in some cases, participants' prior intuition more than a realistic human-AI decision-making setting."

**The load-bearing assumption: "same-instance-repetition-equals-explanation-effect."** The two-phase same-instance appropriate-reliance metric treats any change (or maintenance) of the participant's judgment between phase-1 and phase-2 as attributable to the AI advice and explanation shown in phase-2. But the very act of judging instance X in phase-1 creates a prior judgment that persists into phase-2 through anchoring [13, 14, 15], commitment-and-consistency [16, 17], and mere-exposure familiarity [18]. The phase-2 reading is therefore a composite of three distinct sources of variation: (1) what the explanation and AI advice genuinely contribute (R), (2) what the measurement design contributes through same-instance repetition (P), and (3) their interaction (RxP), which concentrates on the human-AI disagreement cells the field analyzes most closely.

**Decomposition: R / P / RxP.** The RERC profile decomposes the single two-phase reliance scalar into three operationally defined components:

- *R (genuine revision):* The portion of the appropriate-reliance metric attributable to AI advice and explanation alone, estimated from participants in a between-subject fresh-instance arm who encounter each instance for the first time with AI advice -- no phase-1 anchor exists. This is the design-clean readout.
- *P (prior-commitment artifact):* The portion of the measured reliance that exists only because the participant judged the same instance unaided in phase-1 -- the anchoring/commitment increment. Estimated as the within-subject-minus-between-subject design-arm contrast, controlling for instance and participant via crossed random effects. Vanishes by definition under fresh single-exposure.
- *RxP (commitment-by-disagreement interaction, separately labeled):* The prior-commitment artifact concentrates on the human-AI disagreement instances the source analyzes separately: where the phase-1 anchor is maximally dissonant with AI advice, commitment pressure is strongest. Estimated as the design-factor x agreement-status interaction in the GLMM. Never folded into R or P.

**Pressure-testing rival reframes.** Three alternative explanations for the same-instance-versus-fresh-instance difference must be ruled out:

1. *"It is just an order/practice effect -- the second pass is easier."* This is handled by counterbalanced instance order, matched phase-1 exposure time across all commitment conditions, and filler items that break the repetition pattern. The anchor-destroying placebo (Leg-1) further isolates the same-instance linkage: participants still complete a full phase-1 block (controlling for fatigue, engagement, and practice) but judge DIFFERENT instances in phase-2. If the effect were purely order/practice, Leg-1 would show the same increment as the standard same-instance arm.
2. *"The anchor is on the AI advice, not the participant's own prior."* Rastogi et al. [19] modeled anchoring on the AI's output. RERC targets a different anchor: the participant's OWN phase-1 judgment, which is created by the measurement design. The advice-absent placebo (Leg-2) separates the two: phase-2 shows an explanation-shaped scaffold but NO AI verdict, so there is nothing to anchor on from the advice side. R must drop to approximately zero under Leg-2, confirming that the advice channel is isolated. The two anchoring sources (design-induced prior and advice-induced) are distinct and can be estimated jointly.
3. *"Explanations genuinely help, and the fresh-instance arm underestimates the true effect."* RERC does not deny that explanations help -- genuine revision is captured in R. The question is whether the two-phase metric OVER-estimates this effect by adding P and RxP. If the fresh-instance arm yields an R equal to the two-phase scalar, both placebos are null, and no commitment dose-response is detected, then the two-phase design is vindicated and RERC is empirically wrong. This is the pre-committed refutation.

**Baseline ladder B1-B4.**

- *B1 (collapse target = source recomputed):* The two-phase same-instance appropriate-reliance rate computed under the source's measurement condition (within-subject, committed-and-recorded, real explanation, zero inter-phase lag). By construction, equals the collapse output within floating-point tolerance; reproduces the source's published metric within reproduction tolerance.
- *B2 (fresh-instance correction):* Add the between-subject fresh-instance arm. First R-vs-P separation. The difference B2-minus-B1 estimates the net design-artifact bias in the source's reliance metric.
- *B3 (full commitment-decomposed profile):* Full crossed design-factor x commitment-level x real-vs-placebo GLMM with random effects over participant x instance x design-factor. Variance components emit R, P, and the separately labeled RxP. Includes graded commitment manipulation and dual-placebo controls.
- *B4 (oracle = upper bound only):* A normative "no-prior, ground-truth-AI-correctness" maximum-appropriate-reliance benchmark -- the reliance a rational Bayesian agent would exhibit given a single fresh exposure and knowledge of the AI's calibrated accuracy. Upper bound only; the within-study R-vs-P-vs-RxP decomposition is the primary estimator.

**Falsifiability triad.**

1. *By-construction collapse (unit-testable):* Under three limiting parameters -- design factor = within-subject same-instance only, commitment = committed-and-recorded, variance decomposition marginalized to grand-mean readout -- the R/P/RxP profile equals the recomputed two-phase scalar within floating-point tolerance. Automated unit test in the analysis pipeline.
2. *Dual/two-leg placebo (both legs required):* Leg-1 (anchor-destroying): scramble phase-1-to-phase-2 instance mapping; P must collapse toward zero. Leg-2 (advice-absent): remove the AI verdict while keeping an explanation-shaped scaffold; R must drop to approximately zero.
3. *Commitment dose-response dissociation:* As commitment strength rises (exposed-only -> private -> committed-and-recorded), the within-subject same-instance metric must diverge monotonically from the fresh-instance metric (P and RxP grow; R stays flat). Pre-committed refutation: if both metrics track one-for-one, both placebos are null, and no dose effect is detected, the two-phase design is innocuous and RERC is empirically wrong.

**Summary of the collective idea.** The Repeated-Exposure Reliance Confound (RERC) identifies a measurement-design artifact in the standard two-phase same-instance paradigm used to evaluate whether AI explanations promote appropriate reliance. The artifact arises because judging the same instance twice -- unaided in phase-1, then with AI advice in phase-2 -- creates a prior-commitment anchor that distorts the phase-2 reliance reading through anchoring, commitment-and-consistency, and mere-exposure mechanisms. RERC decomposes the confounded scalar into a design-clean genuine-revision component (R), a prior-commitment artifact (P), and a separately labeled commitment-by-disagreement interaction (RxP), with a by-construction collapse identity that ensures the profile contains the old metric as a special case. A between-subject fresh-instance arm, graded commitment manipulations, and a dual-placebo design make each component independently estimable and falsifiable. The result is a reusable methodological audit instrument for the entire class of two-phase XAI reliance studies, with a pre-committed refutation criterion that would vindicate the existing measurement convention if the confound proves negligible.

## 3. Novel Insights and Recommendations

**1. Attribution, not invalidation.** RERC does not argue that two-phase reliance metrics are wrong; it argues they are UNDECOMPOSED. The R/P/RxP profile attributes the measured reliance change to its sources -- explanation content (R), measurement-design reactivity (P), and their interaction on disagreement items (RxP) -- and shows that the standard scalar is the special case obtained when P and RxP are marginalized away. This framing makes the contribution additive (the profile contains the old number) rather than adversarial (the old number is wrong), and it makes adoption incentive-compatible: researchers can report the profile alongside the conventional metric without disowning prior results.

**2. Collapse-anchored decomposition and the honest near-miss.** The methodology of within-subject testing threats is well established: Campbell and Stanley [22] catalogued it in 1963; Shadish, Cook, and Campbell [23] formalized it in 2002; Charness, Gneezy, and Kuhn [24] demonstrated its empirical consequences for within-subject designs in experimental economics. Anchoring and commitment effects are mature psychology (Tversky and Kahneman [13]; Cialdini [16]). The near-miss is that these literatures have developed in parallel with the XAI reliance subfield, and no prior work has combined them into a formal decomposition with a by-construction collapse identity. RERC bridges the silo by porting the testing-threat framework to the XAI measurement algebra, adding the collapse anchor that makes it backward-compatible and the dual-placebo triad that makes it falsifiable.

**3. Identifying P without ground-truth "true belief."** The hardest conceptual challenge is that P -- the prior-commitment artifact -- cannot be estimated by comparing the participant's phase-2 answer to their "true belief," because no such ground truth exists. RERC resolves this by defining P as an operational design contrast (within-subject same-instance minus between-subject fresh-instance), not as a latent psychological trait. The fresh-instance arm provides the design-clean counterfactual: what would the participant's reliance behavior look like if they encountered the instance for the first time with AI advice? The three-level commitment manipulation (committed-and-recorded / private / exposed-only) then provides a dose-response gradient that is consistent with commitment theory but does not require the design to recover unobservable cognitive processes. This operational honesty -- defining components by the contrasts that estimate them, not by the mechanisms they are "supposed" to reflect -- is what makes RERC falsifiable rather than merely interpretive.

**Responsible AI considerations.** The proposed study is a pre-registered, IRB-approved human-subjects experiment. All participants provide informed consent prior to participation. The placebo and advice-absent arms involve mild deception (an explanation-shaped scaffold with no real AI verdict, or a scrambled phase-1-to-phase-2 instance mapping); these require explicit post-study debriefing guaranteeing that no participant leaves with lasting misinformation about the AI's predictions or the decision tasks. The prediction tasks (recidivism risk using COMPAS/ProPublica data [27] and income prediction using the UCI Adult dataset [30]) are administered as research stimuli, not deployed decision-support tools; participants are informed that their judgments have no real-world consequences. The COMPAS dataset is a canonical case study in algorithmic fairness with extensively documented racial disparities in recidivism prediction (Angwin et al. 2016 [27]); the UCI Adult dataset similarly encodes demographic correlations (e.g., income with race and gender). Both are used here strictly as research stimuli for studying human reliance behavior, not as endorsement of algorithmic recidivism or income prediction for deployment; any downstream application should account for these representational-harm concerns. Demographic data are collected at the aggregate level only; no personally identifiable information is retained. Post-study awareness probes (checking whether participants detected the same-instance repetition or the placebo manipulation) are pre-registered with explicit exclusion criteria for sensitivity analysis. Any extension to higher-stakes domains (clinical, legal) would require separate ethics approval and is scoped as future work, not as deployment evidence from this study.

---

Here are the five core questions:

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

When the appropriate-reliance metric reported for explanation-aided human-AI decision-making -- the agreement-based reliance rate, switch fraction, or weight-of-advice measured in a two-phase same-instance design where the participant first judges instance X unaided and then re-judges the SAME instance X with AI advice and explanation -- conflates genuine explanation-driven belief revision, a prior-commitment anchoring artifact created by the phase-1 unaided judgment on that same instance, and their interaction concentrated on the human-AI disagreement cases where the phase-1 anchor is maximally dissonant with the AI advice, does promoting that single two-phase scalar to a design-controlled, commitment-decomposed appropriate-reliance profile -- measured by adding a between-subject fresh-instance single-exposure arm (each participant sees each instance exactly once, with AI, and no phase-1 anchor) together with graded commitment manipulations and dual-placebo controls, decomposed by crossed mixed-effects variance components over participant, instance, and design-factor into a genuine-revision component R, a prior-commitment artifact component P, and a separately labeled commitment-by-disagreement interaction component RxP, and constructed so that under the within-subject same-instance committed-and-recorded real-explanation zero-lag limit with the design factor marginalized to a single grand-mean readout the profile collapses to the original two-phase appropriate-reliance scalar as recomputed within floating-point tolerance -- reveal that the reported reliance effect is partly or wholly attributable to the anchoring artifact of repeated same-instance exposure and its interaction with human-AI disagreement rather than to genuine explanation-driven belief revision?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

RERC is interesting and important because it targets a measurement CONVENTION, not an isolated methodological choice. The two-phase same-instance design -- in which the participant judges an instance unaided and then re-judges the same instance with AI advice -- is the default instrument across the XAI decision-support subfield. It underpins the weight-of-advice metric by algebraic necessity (the formula requires a pre-advice estimate on the same item [11]), the switch-fraction metric by data-flow necessity (one must know the pre-advice answer to detect a switch), and the agreement-based appropriate-reliance rate by experimental convention (phase-1 collects the anchor against which phase-2 agreement is assessed). Correcting a confound in this design therefore propagates across every study that uses any of these metrics, making RERC a subfield-scale methodological audit rather than a single-study critique.

Second, RERC reframes the mixed-results crisis. The meta-analytic inconsistency documented by Schemmer et al. 2022 [8] -- in which explanations sometimes improve, sometimes harm, and sometimes leave unchanged human-AI decision quality -- has been attributed to variation in explanation type, task difficulty, and participant expertise. RERC offers an additional, previously unlabeled source of variance: design-reactivity heterogeneity. Different studies implement the two-phase design with different commitment levels (publicly recorded vs. privately noted vs. merely contemplated), different inter-phase lags (immediate vs. delayed), and different instructions about whether the phase-1 answer "counts." Each of these modulates the anchoring artifact P and the interaction RxP independently of the explanation. To the extent that design-reactivity variance masquerades as effect heterogeneity, the mixed-results picture is partly artifactual, and the R/P/RxP decomposition is the diagnostic that separates genuine from spurious variation.

Third, the interaction component RxP is sharpest on the human-AI DISAGREEMENT instances that the subfield scrutinizes most closely. Appropriate-reliance metrics gain their diagnostic power from what happens when the human's initial judgment and the AI's advice conflict: does the human appropriately switch (if the AI is correct) or appropriately persist (if the AI is wrong)? These disagreement items are precisely the cases where the phase-1 anchor is maximally dissonant with the AI advice, so commitment-and-consistency pressure is strongest. The confound therefore contaminates the most-scrutinized cell of the data -- the one that drives headline claims about whether explanations help achieve appropriate reliance.

Fourth, RERC yields a cheap, reusable methodological reform. Any study using the two-phase same-instance design can add a between-subject fresh-instance arm -- in which each participant sees each instance exactly once, with AI advice, and no phase-1 anchor -- to obtain the design-clean readout R. The difference between the two-phase metric and R estimates the net design-artifact bias. This is a low-cost addition (one extra experimental condition) that can be appended to new studies or used retrospectively as an audit instrument: given a published study's reported two-phase metric and a fresh-instance replication, the R/P/RxP profile can be computed to assess how much of the reported effect survives the removal of the same-instance repetition.

Fifth, the collapse identity guarantees backward compatibility. By construction the R/P/RxP profile, when evaluated under the within-subject same-instance committed-and-recorded limit with the design factor marginalized to a single grand-mean readout, reproduces the original two-phase scalar within floating-point tolerance. The profile therefore CONTAINS the old number as a special case; it does not compete with or contradict published results. This removes a significant adoption barrier: researchers can report the R/P/RxP profile alongside the conventional metric without invalidating their prior publications. The practical stakes are high. Appropriate reliance is the quality criterion for human-AI decision-making in healthcare, criminal justice, and finance, where the cost of overreliance on a wrong AI or underreliance on a correct AI is measured in patient outcomes, liberty, and capital. If the scalar used to certify reliance quality is inflated or deflated by a measurement artifact, deployment decisions made on its basis are correspondingly distorted. RERC does not question the importance of appropriate reliance; it questions whether the number currently used to measure it is trustworthy.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The Repeated-Exposure Reliance Confound (RERC) decomposition demands new empirical data, not reanalysis, and every design decision that makes the decomposition clean also makes the study harder to run. Seven specific difficulties follow.

**1. The design-clean counterfactual requires new data collection.** The fresh-instance single-exposure readout -- the R channel -- cannot be extracted from any existing two-phase dataset. In the source design every participant judged instance X unaided, then re-judged the same X with AI advice; no participant ever encountered an instance for the first time alongside AI advice. A between-subjects fresh-instance arm must therefore recruit an entirely new cohort whose members each see every instance exactly once, with the AI prediction and explanation, and render a single verdict. The collapse identity runs in one direction only: under the within-subject same-instance, committed-and-recorded, grand-mean-marginalized limit the full R/P/RxP profile collapses to the source's two-phase scalar as recomputed within floating-point tolerance. The reverse is impossible -- one cannot invert the source scalar back into the three-component profile because R and P are design-identified contrasts that require both arms to estimate.

**2. Three psychological mechanisms, not one contrast.** A naive same-instance-versus-fresh-instance comparison would lump three distinct drivers of the prior-commitment artifact: (a) anchoring-and-adjustment [13] -- the phase-1 verdict serves as a numeric anchor for the phase-2 revision; (b) commitment-and-consistency pressure [16] -- having publicly recorded a judgment, the participant resists changing it; (c) mere-exposure familiarity [18] -- seeing the instance twice increases fluency and inflates confidence. Separating these requires a graded three-level commitment manipulation (committed-and-recorded / elicited-but-private / exposed-only-no-judgment) plus a sham-phase-1 control in which the participant views the instance but answers an unrelated question. Each level adds a cell; at three commitment levels crossed with two design arms, two explanation conditions, and a meaningful set of instances, the cell count and required sample size grow rapidly.

**3. Constructing a valid anchor-destroying placebo (Leg-1).** The scrambled phase-1-to-phase-2 mapping must hold time-on-task, cognitive fatigue, and task engagement constant while removing only the instance-specific anchor. Participants complete a full phase-1 block -- same number of items, same response mode, same duration -- but the instances they encounter in phase-2 are drawn from a disjoint set. The scramble must be undetectable: if participants notice that phase-2 items are new, demand characteristics shift (they may infer the study is about repetition effects). Validation requires a post-study suspicion probe and, ideally, a pilot confirming that detection rates are indistinguishable from chance.

**4. Demand characteristics and Hawthorne effects in the same-instance arm.** Participants who recognize that they are re-judging the same instances may consciously "perform reliance" -- either stubbornly sticking with their phase-1 answer or performatively switching to show open-mindedness. A neutral cover story (e.g., "we are calibrating AI explanations across different display formats") is necessary, along with filler items that break the perceptual pattern of exact repetition. Post-study awareness probes must be pre-registered with explicit exclusion criteria: any participant who reports noticing the same-instance repetition and believing the study is about consistency is flagged for sensitivity analysis.

**5. Between-arm instance equivalence.** Instances in the fresh-instance arm must be matched in difficulty, base-rate correctness, and AI-model accuracy to the instances in the same-instance arm. Without stratification and counterbalancing -- e.g., a Latin-square rotation so that every instance appears in both arms across participants -- a difficulty imbalance aliases directly onto P: the fresh-instance arm could appear to show higher or lower reliance simply because its instances are easier or harder. Stratification by AI-correctness (AI right vs. AI wrong) is load-bearing because the RxP interaction is defined on disagreement cells.

**6. Statistical power for the RxP interaction.** RxP is a design-factor-by-agreement-status (by commitment-level) interaction variance component estimated in a crossed generalized linear mixed model (GLMM) with random effects for participant, instance, and design-factor. Interactions require roughly four times the sample size needed for the corresponding main effect [33]. Moreover, high-order random-effect variances in crossed designs routinely hit boundary estimates or convergence failures in standard mixed-model software [35]. The honest consequence is that RxP may be only bounded -- not precisely estimated -- at feasible sample sizes (~300-500 per arm). The study must therefore report minimum detectable effect size for RxP a priori via simulation [34], accept that the interaction may be directionally interpretable but wide-CI, and pre-register a fallback aggregation if the full crossed model does not converge.

**7. Identifiability honesty.** R, P, and RxP are operational design contrasts, not recovered latent psychological traits. The design identifies a between-arm difference (P) and its moderation by agreement status (RxP); it does not uniquely map these onto anchoring versus commitment versus mere-exposure at the cognitive-process level. Over-claiming mechanism separation the design cannot deliver would undermine the paper's methodological credibility. The three-level commitment manipulation provides a dissociation consistent with commitment-and-consistency theory, but it does not eliminate alternative explanations at the process level. This limitation must be stated explicitly.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

Five concrete barriers explain why the RERC decomposition has not been constructed despite the individual ingredients being available.

**B1: The confound-creating feature is the feature researchers want.** The two-phase same-instance design is the field's default instrument not by accident but by design preference. It enables within-subject comparisons that are statistically powerful (each participant serves as their own control), ecologically plausible (advisory interactions in practice often involve the advisor seeing a case the decision-maker has already reviewed), and -- most importantly -- it enables the agree/disagree split that is the backbone of appropriate-reliance analysis. Abandoning it wholesale would discard the very data structure (initial judgment vs. revised judgment on the SAME instance) that defines switch fraction, weight-of-advice, and the agreement-based appropriate-reliance metrics. RERC does not ask the field to abandon this design; it asks the field to ADD a fresh-instance arm that provides the design-clean counterfactual. But until the confound was named, there was no reason to incur the cost of an additional experimental condition for a measurement artifact that had not been identified as a measurement artifact.

**B2: The design-clean counterfactual sits outside the standard single-cohort protocol.** The "R" component of the RERC profile requires a between-subject fresh-instance arm in which participants encounter each instance for the first time with AI advice. This arm cannot be obtained from the same participants who completed the standard two-phase protocol: once a participant has judged instance X unaided in phase-1, their phase-2 response to X is contaminated by that judgment. The fresh-instance readout therefore requires a SEPARATE cohort of participants (or a carefully counterbalanced split-sample design with non-overlapping instance sets). This doubles the participant budget compared to a standard single-cohort study. In a field where crowdsourced participant recruitment is already expensive and power calculations are already tight (the key RxP interaction is a three-way term requiring substantial sample sizes), the cost of an extra between-subjects arm is a real practical barrier. No one builds a control condition for a confound they have not yet named.

**B3: The relevant literatures are siloed.** The psychology of anchoring, commitment, and mere-exposure is a mature subfield of judgment and decision-making research. The methodology of within-subject testing threats is a staple of research-methods textbooks [22, 23]. The XAI decision-support literature is a fast-growing subfield of human-computer interaction and AI. These three communities have largely developed in parallel. Anchoring has been studied in human-AI teams (Rastogi et al. [19]), but the anchor modeled is the AI's advice, not the participant's own prior judgment from the measurement design. Testing threats have been applied to educational interventions and clinical trials, but not ported to XAI reliance metrics. Commitment-and-consistency is invoked as a general psychological principle but not formalized as a variance component in the XAI measurement algebra. The disciplinary gap between psychology/methodology and HCI/XAI meant that no single research group was positioned to see the two-phase same-instance design as a testing threat, model the testing threat with anchoring and commitment theory, and decompose the reliance metric accordingly.

**B4: Flagging is cheap; decomposing is expensive.** Multiple papers in the XAI reliance literature flag the same-instance repetition as a limitation. The source paper notes that its design "may have strengthened prior intuition." Bucinca et al. [7] acknowledge within-subject carryover. But a limitations paragraph costs one sentence and no additional data collection. QUANTIFYING the confound requires a factorial design (same-instance vs. fresh-instance, crossed with commitment level and explanation type), variance-component estimation via crossed random-effects models, a dual-placebo control (anchor-destroying and advice-absent legs), and a pre-committed refutation criterion. The methodological investment is an order of magnitude larger than the cost of flagging, and the payoff is uncertain until the decomposition is actually run: the confound might turn out to be negligible, in which case the paper "merely" validates the existing measurement convention (a useful but unglamorous result).

**B5: Without the collapse identity, an alternative metric appears to compete with published numbers.** Proposing a new measurement of appropriate reliance that yields different numbers from the established two-phase metric creates an incentive problem: published studies that reported the old metric would appear to be contradicted, and the new metric would be seen as an attack on prior work rather than an extension of it. This is an unattractive position for researchers who need citations and collaborations from the very community whose numbers they would be revising. RERC's by-construction collapse identity removes this disincentive. Because the R/P/RxP profile is constructed so that under the within-subject same-instance limit it reproduces the original two-phase scalar exactly, the profile is a STRICT SUPERSET of the old metric: it contains the old number as a limiting case and adds the decomposition on top. Researchers can adopt the profile without disowning their prior results. No prior proposal offered this backward-compatibility guarantee, making alternative metrics appear adversarial rather than additive.

Taken together, these five barriers explain why the ingredients for RERC -- anchoring theory, commitment psychology, testing threats, appropriate-reliance metrics -- have coexisted for years without being combined into a decomposition. RERC differs from prior work in three specific ways: (1) it decomposes rather than merely critiques, yielding quantitative R/P/RxP variance components rather than a qualitative limitations paragraph; (2) it is backward-compatible via the collapse identity, ensuring the profile contains rather than contradicts the established metric; and (3) it isolates R, P, and RxP by design contrasts (fresh-instance vs. same-instance, commitment-level manipulation, placebo controls) rather than by post hoc statistical adjustment, making each component independently falsifiable.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

The study is a pre-registered, IRB-approved human-subjects experiment run online (Prolific) with the following concrete components.

**Tasks.** Two binary classification prediction tasks, chosen to mirror the source paper's "two prediction tasks" and high-stakes decision flavor, each with a publicly available dataset, established AI models, and verifiable ground truth:

1. *Recidivism risk prediction (COMPAS / ProPublica).* Participants predict whether a defendant will re-offend within two years given a demographic-and-criminal-history profile. The ProPublica COMPAS dataset [27] is the standard benchmark; Dressel and Farid 2018 [28] and Green and Chen 2019 [29] used it in human-AI reliance studies, establishing both a human baseline (~65% accuracy) and a calibrated AI baseline (~65-67%). Ground truth is the two-year re-arrest indicator.

2. *Income prediction (UCI Adult / Census-Income).* Participants predict whether an individual earns above or below $50K/year given demographic and employment features. The UCI Adult dataset [30] has been used in human-AI deferral/reliance work (e.g., Mozannar and Sontag 2020 [31]; Bansal et al. [3]). A gradient-boosted-trees model achieves ~86% accuracy; ground truth is the income bracket.

Both tasks are binary, high-stakes-flavored, and permit clean AI-correct vs AI-incorrect stratification. They are administered as 40-item blocks (20 per task, counterbalanced), plus 8 filler items per block (unrelated trivia questions) to disrupt repetition detection.

**AI advisor and explanations.**

- *Model:* A pre-trained gradient-boosted classifier (XGBoost) for each task, calibrated via Platt scaling so that stated confidences match empirical accuracy. Model accuracy is disclosed to participants ("this AI is correct about X% of the time overall").
- *Real explanation:* SHAP-based feature-attribution bar chart [32], showing the top-5 feature contributions to the AI prediction. This is the dominant explanation type in the XAI appropriate-reliance literature.
- *Placebo explanation (Leg-2 falsifiability):* A visually identical bar chart with the same layout, number of bars, color scheme, and bar lengths, but features are randomly reassigned from the instance's feature vector (shuffled attribution). The explanation-shaped scaffold carries no veridical signal about the prediction.
- *Advice-absent condition (Leg-2 dual placebo):* The participant sees the instance profile plus an explanation-shaped scaffold (gray placeholder bars, same layout and cognitive load) but NO AI prediction verdict. There is nothing to rely on, so R must drop to approximately zero.

**Experimental design.** A mixed factorial: 2 (design: within-subject same-instance vs between-subject fresh-instance) x 3 (commitment level: committed-and-recorded / elicited-but-private / exposed-only-no-judgment) x 2 (explanation: real SHAP vs placebo/sham), with agreement status (AI-agrees vs AI-disagrees with participant or ground truth) as a crossed within-instance factor.

- *Within-subject same-instance arm* replicates the source's two-phase design: phase-1 unaided judgment on instance X, then phase-2 re-judgment on the same X with AI advice and explanation.
- *Between-subject fresh-instance arm:* each participant sees every instance exactly once, with AI advice and explanation, and renders a single verdict. No phase-1 exposure.
- *Commitment manipulation (within-subject arm only):* Committed-and-recorded (the source's design: participant types and submits a prediction plus confidence rating); elicited-but-private (participant enters a prediction via slider but is told "this is a practice round, your answer will not be recorded"); exposed-only-no-judgment (participant views the instance for a matched duration and answers an unrelated engagement question; no prediction is made).
- *Sham phase-1 control:* for the exposed-only condition, the participant sees the instance but never forms a prediction, controlling for familiarity without anchoring.
- *Inter-phase lag conditions (within-subject arm):* 0-minute (immediate, the source's design), 30-minute (short delay), and 24-hour (overnight delay) to test anchor decay. The 0-minute condition is the B1 replication cell; lag conditions are secondary.
- *Instance stratification:* a Latin-square rotation ensures every instance appears in both design arms across participants. Instances are pre-stratified by AI correctness (AI-right / AI-wrong) and task difficulty (easy / hard, based on pilot human accuracy).
- *Filler items:* 8 per block, unrelated to the prediction task, to disrupt repetition awareness.
- *Sample-size target:* N = 480 per design arm (960 total), yielding ~160 per commitment-level cell in the within-subject arm. A priori power simulation (simr R package [34], 1000 iterations) targets 80% power for a minimum detectable RxP interaction effect of Cohen's d = 0.25 (small-to-medium), assuming ICC = 0.10 for participant and instance random effects in a logistic GLMM.

**Readouts and metrics.**

1. *Agreement-based appropriate-reliance metrics:* Relative AI Reliance (RAIR) and Relative Self-Reliance (RSR) per Schemmer et al. [5]; switch fraction (proportion of cases where participant changes from phase-1 to phase-2 verdict); decision accuracy.
2. *Over/under-reliance on disagreement cells:* computed separately for AI-correct-disagreement (under-reliance if participant sticks) and AI-incorrect-disagreement (over-reliance if participant switches to wrong AI answer).
3. *Variance components R, P, RxP:* estimated from a crossed GLMM (glmmTMB [35], logit link for binary outcomes) with random intercepts and slopes for participant, instance, and design-factor. R = fresh-instance-arm fixed effect; P = within-minus-between design-arm contrast; RxP = design-arm x agreement-status interaction.
4. *Collapse-identity residual:* under the within-subject, committed-and-recorded, zero-lag, marginalized limit, the profile must equal the recomputed two-phase scalar within floating-point tolerance (< 1e-12 absolute difference). This is a unit test, run on every analysis pipeline execution.

**Baseline ladder B1-B4.**

- *B1 (collapse target = source recomputed):* Two-phase same-instance appropriate-reliance rate computed our way under within-subject, committed-and-recorded, real explanation, zero-lag. Equals the collapse output within floating-point tolerance; reproduces the source's published metric within reproduction tolerance.
- *B2 (fresh-instance correction):* Add between-subject fresh-instance arm. First R-vs-P separation. Report B2-minus-B1 as the net design-artifact bias in the source's reliance metric.
- *B3 (full profile):* Full crossed GLMM emitting R, P, RxP with 95% CIs. Includes commitment-level moderation and dual-placebo controls.
- *B4 (oracle = upper bound only):* Normative no-prior ground-truth-AI-correctness ceiling. A rational Bayesian agent with knowledge of the AI's calibrated accuracy and no anchoring produces the maximum-appropriate-reliance benchmark. Upper bound only; the within-study R-vs-P-vs-RxP decomposition is the primary estimator.

**Falsifiability triad.**

1. *By-construction collapse (unit test):* Under the three limiting parameters, the profile equals the recomputed two-phase scalar within floating-point tolerance. Automated test in the analysis pipeline; failure means the construction is wrong.
2. *Dual placebo (both legs required):* Leg-1 (anchor-destroying): scrambled phase-1-to-phase-2 instance mapping; participants complete a full phase-1 block but judge disjoint instances in phase-2; P must collapse toward zero. Leg-2 (advice-absent): phase-2 shows the explanation-shaped scaffold but no AI verdict; R must drop to approximately zero.
3. *Commitment dose-response dissociation:* As commitment rises (exposed-only -> private -> committed-and-recorded), the within-subject same-instance metric must diverge monotonically from the fresh-instance metric. P and RxP grow with commitment; R stays flat. Pre-committed refutation: if same-instance and fresh-instance metrics track one-for-one across all commitment levels, both placebo legs are null, and no dose effect is detected, then RERC is empirically wrong.

**Expected outcomes.** We predict a substantial positive P (estimated d = 0.20-0.40): the two-phase same-instance scalar over-states appropriate reliance relative to the fresh-instance single-exposure readout. The bias is largest on AI-correct disagreement cells (where appropriate reliance means switching TO the AI), because the phase-1 anchor resists exactly the revision that the appropriate-reliance metric rewards. RxP is predicted positive and concentrated on disagreement cells (estimated d = 0.15-0.30). R (genuine explanation-driven revision) is predicted smaller than the two-phase composite -- the source's reported reliance effect is partly attributable to the anchoring artifact. The clean null that would refute RERC: B2-minus-B1 is indistinguishable from zero (95% CI includes zero), both placebo legs null (P does not drop under scramble; R does not drop under advice-absent), and no commitment dose-response -- the two-phase design is innocuous.

### Note on identifiers

DOIs for references [1]-[15], [17]-[20], [24]-[25] are confidently asserted from known publication records. The DOI for [16] is replaced by an ISBN (Cialdini's textbook). References [21], [22], [23], [26] are given with author-year-title-venue; residual uncertainty about exact volume, pages, or DOI is marked with "(best-known)" where applicable. References [27]-[35] continue from Parker's methodology sections; [33] is flagged as an informal but widely cited source.
